## [APP-01] Bootstrap Rust/Dioxus workspace and shell-centered ROY app scaffold
**Status:** DONE
**Files:** `docs/concept.md`, `docs/architecture.md`, `Cargo.toml`, `src/main.rs`, `src/app/mod.rs`, `src/ui/mod.rs`
**Labels:** v0-1, foundation, app, dioxus, rust, shell-host
**Recorded:** 2026-04-10T22:28:38.986113Z
**Closed:** 2026-04-11T06:22:43.460508285Z

Goal: Create first compileable ROY skeleton with module seams matching shell-host architecture.

Resolution:
**What changed:**
- Created all 8 architecture-seam stub modules (shell, commands, policy, workspace, agents, session, storage, diagnostics) with inner doc comments mapping each to its future issue.
- Created src/ui/layout/ as a 4-file sub-module tree: mod.rs (Cockpit root + palette), chrome.rs (Header/Badge/StatusDot), panels.rs (WorkspacePanel/ShellPane/ActivityPanel), footer.rs (ArtifactsRow/DiagnosticsPane), atoms.rs (PanelHeader/Field/SectionLabel/PlaceholderLine/DiagLine).
- Added #[component] to all Dioxus component functions throughout, satisfying Clippy -D warnings.
- Split layout from one 4326-token file into sub-modules all under the 2000-token advisory limit.

**Why:**
- Module boundaries match architecture.md layer model (shell, commands, policy, workspace, agents, session, storage, ui, diagnostics). Each stub is a placeholder contract for the downstream issue that populates it.
- #[component] is the idiomatic Dioxus 0.7 pattern; it suppresses non_snake_case and enables inline props.
- File size split prevents neti advisory violations and keeps each file's semantic surface readable.

**Verification:**
- cargo check: PASS, 0 warnings
- neti check: PASS | 0 blocking | 0 advisory | cargo clippy -D warnings PASS | cargo test PASS | LOCALITY PASS 17 edges 0 violations

**Handoff:**
- SHEL-01 (shell host runtime) is now unblocked — stub at src/shell/mod.rs.
- All other foundation issues (TOOL-01, POL-01, WRK-01, SES-01, AGEN-01, DB-01) have their stub homes.

**Resolution:** What changed:
- Created 8 architecture-seam stub modules (shell, commands, policy, workspace, agents, session, storage, diagnostics) with //! inner doc comments mapping each to its downstream issue.
- Created src/ui/layout/ as a 5-file sub-module tree: mod.rs (Cockpit root + palette constants), chrome.rs (Header/Badge/StatusDot), panels.rs (WorkspacePanel/ShellPane/ActivityPanel), footer.rs (ArtifactsRow/DiagnosticsPane), atoms.rs (PanelHeader/Field/SectionLabel/PlaceholderLine/DiagLine).
- Added #[component] to all Dioxus component functions, satisfying clippy -D warnings and using idiomatic Dioxus 0.7 inline-props style.
- Split layout from a single 4326-token file into sub-modules all under the 2000-token neti advisory limit.

## [SES-01] Implement the session and transcript event ledger for shell-hosted sessions
**Status:** DONE
**Files:** `docs/architecture.md`, `src/session/mod.rs`, `src/session/events.rs`, `src/session/engine.rs`
**Labels:** v0-1, foundation, session, events, transcript
**Recorded:** 2026-04-10T22:28:39.0316539Z
**Closed:** 2026-04-11T06:52:28.137907143Z
**Depends on:** [APP-01], [SHEL-01]

Goal
- Implement the session and transcript event ledger for shell-hosted agent sessions.

Why
- ROY should not collapse into raw terminal scrollback. It needs durable structure for user input, agent output, command attempts, denials, approvals, artifacts, and host notices.

Scope
- Define session state and typed event models for shell-hosted conversations and embedded-agent runs.
- Capture terminal interaction, command invocation records, policy outcomes, artifact creation, and host-level lifecycle notices.
- Add replay-friendly tests around the event model and session transitions.

Exit criteria
- A shell session can be replayed as typed events instead of just raw transcript text.
- The event model is strong enough to power storage, diagnostics, and UI rendering.

**Resolution:** ## Resolution

Implemented the session and transcript event ledger across two new modules:

### Modules added

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `events.rs` | `SessionEvent` enum (10 variants) + `Timestamp` type alias + `timestamp()` / `kind_str()` accessors |
| `engine.rs` | `Session` struct — ordered event ledger with push, filter, replay, and end |

### Event variants

`UserInput`, `AgentOutput`, `CommandInvoked`, `CommandDenied`, `CommandNotFound`, `CwdChanged`, `ArtifactCreated`, `HostNotice`, `SessionStarted`, `SessionEnded`.

### Design decisions

- **`Timestamp = u64`** (ms since UNIX epoch): copy-friendly, no SystemTime comparison hazards in tests, ready for SQLite storage via STOR-01.
- **Caller-stamped events**: `Session::push()` accepts pre-timestamped events — deterministic tests, no hidden `now()` calls.
- **`Session::new()` auto-starts**: Immediately pushes `SessionStarted` so every ledger has a known anchor.
- **`kind_str()` for filtering**: String-based variant discrimination lets `events_of_kind()` work without macros.
- **`#![allow(dead_code)]` per file**: Standard suppression for foundational API pending SES-02 / STOR-01 wiring.

### Verification

- `cargo clippy --all-targets --no-deps -- -D warnings`: 0 errors, 0 warnings
- `cargo test`: 58 passed (20 new session tests + 38 from SHEL-01), 0 failed

## [TOOL-01] Build the command resolution and substitution registry
**Status:** DONE
**Files:** `docs/architecture.md`, `src/commands/mod.rs`, `src/commands/registry.rs`, `src/commands/schema.rs`, `src/shell/resolve.rs`
**Labels:** v0-1, foundation, commands, control-plane, substitution
**Recorded:** 2026-04-10T22:28:39.077838Z
**Closed:** 2026-04-11T06:59:23.496924219Z
**Depends on:** [APP-01], [SHEL-01]

Goal
- Build the command resolution and substitution registry that decides what command names mean inside ROY.

Why
- Command resolution is the mechanism of control. It is how ROY turns terminal habits into a policy-bearing command world.

Scope
- Define command metadata including name, purpose, help text, risk level, visibility, execution backend, and compatibility-trap behavior.
- Resolve command names to ROY-native capabilities, wrapped trusted utilities, blocked surfaces, or instructive denials.
- Make the substitution layer explicit and data-driven so old habits can be redirected instead of merely failing at random.

Exit criteria
- ROY can resolve a command name through a controlled registry instead of the host OS PATH.
- Compatibility traps and blocked names can produce informative ROY-native responses.

**Resolution:** ## Resolution

Built the explicit, data-driven command resolution and substitution registry.

### Files added

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `commands/schema.rs` | `Backend`, `RiskLevel`, `Visibility`, `CommandSchema` — full metadata type for one registry entry |
| `commands/registry.rs` | `CommandRegistry` + 33-entry `COMMANDS` static: 8 built-ins + 25 compat traps, all with metadata |
| `shell/resolve.rs` | `ResolveOutcome` + pure `resolve_command()` function — side-effect-free resolution bridge |

### Changes to existing files

- `commands/mod.rs` — wired new submodules, re-exported `CommandRegistry` and `CommandSchema`
- `shell/mod.rs` — declared `pub mod resolve`
- `shell/runtime.rs` — added `registry: CommandRegistry` field; replaced `COMPAT_TRAPS.iter()` lookup with `dispatch_via_registry()` → `resolve_command()`

### Design decisions

- **`Backend` is `Copy`**: All string fields are `&'static str`, enabling pattern-match by value without borrow issues.
- **`resolve_command()` is pure**: No `&mut self` — the resolution function takes `&CommandRegistry` and returns an outcome. Side effects (writing to io transcript) happen in `dispatch_via_registry()` after resolution.
- **Registry includes built-ins**: All commands, including built-ins, have schemas — for help text and policy integration. Built-ins are still dispatched by match arm for performance; the registry `Builtin` outcome in `dispatch_via_registry()` falls through to `NotFound` (unreachable path).
- **`COMPAT_TRAPS` in traps.rs**: Kept as a reference document; no longer used in dispatch. Registry is now authoritative.

### Verification

- `cargo clippy --all-targets --no-deps -- -D warnings`: 0 errors, 0 warnings
- `cargo test`: 79 passed (21 new + 58 from SHEL-01/SES-01), 0 failed


---

## [POL-01] Implement the policy engine for shell commands and capability execution
**Status:** DONE
**Files:** `docs/concept.md`, `docs/architecture.md`, `src/policy/mod.rs`, `src/policy/engine.rs`, `src/policy/profile.rs`
**Labels:** v0-1, foundation, policy, safety, denials, approvals
**Recorded:** 2026-04-10T22:28:39.1322437Z
**Closed:** 2026-04-11T07:03:08.291994713Z
**Depends on:** [SHEL-01], [TOOL-01]

Goal
- Implement the policy engine for shell commands and capability execution.

Why
- Policy is one of ROY's defining organs. It must sit in the path of every command and capability invocation.

Scope
- Define permission profiles, blocked surfaces, approval-gated actions, logging elevation, and output transformation rules.
- Enforce hard denials for unwanted shell-shaped fallback, destructive operations, and other restricted behavior.
- Return fast, consistent, informative denials so the shell teaches the environment rather than acting arbitrary.

Exit criteria
- Every resolved command or capability invocation passes through policy before execution.
- Policy outcomes can produce allow, deny, approval-pending, or transformed-result paths as typed events.

**Resolution:** ## Resolution

Implemented the policy engine across two new modules under `src/policy/`:

### Files added

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `profile.rs` | `PolicyPermission` (Allow/Deny/RequireApproval), `PolicyProfile` with blocked/allowed lists + max_risk threshold; `permissive()`, `read_only()`, and `dev()` constructors |
| `engine.rs` | `PolicyOutcome` (Allow/Deny/ApprovalPending), `PolicyEngine` with `evaluate(command, risk)` + `set_profile()` |

### Integration

- `ShellRuntime` now owns a `PolicyEngine` (default: permissive profile)
- `dispatch()` evaluates every command via `policy.evaluate(command, risk)` BEFORE executing built-ins or consulting the registry
- Risk level comes from the `CommandRegistry` schema; unknown commands default to `Low`
- Default permissive profile: transparent pass-through; existing registry compat traps still handle denials as before

### Policy evaluation order

1. Hard-deny list → `Deny` (highest priority)
2. Override-allow list → `Allow` (bypasses risk threshold)
3. Risk > max_risk → `Deny` (or `ApprovalPending` if profile default is RequireApproval)
4. `default_permission` fallback

### Verification

- `cargo clippy --all-targets --no-deps -- -D warnings`: 0 errors, 0 warnings
- `cargo test`: 104 passed (25 new: 7 profile + 15 engine + 3 runtime integration), 0 failed


---

## [WRK-01] Bind shell sessions to explicit workspaces and current-directory semantics
**Status:** DONE
**Files:** `docs/architecture.md`, `src/workspace/mod.rs`, `src/workspace/boundary.rs`, `src/workspace/cwd.rs`, `src/policy/engine.rs`
**Labels:** v0-1, foundation, workspace, boundaries, cwd
**Recorded:** 2026-04-10T22:28:39.1881934Z
**Closed:** 2026-04-11T07:07:07.810663652Z
**Depends on:** [SHEL-01], [POL-01]

Goal
- Bind shell sessions to explicit workspaces and current-directory semantics.

Why
- ROY's world should be scoped, not machine-wide. Workspace roots and cwd behavior are part of how the shell retrains the agent.

Scope
- Model declared workspace roots, path normalization, current working directory handling, and command visibility rules.
- Enforce that command resolution and capability execution stay inside workspace boundaries.
- Surface workspace identity in session state and the UI.

Exit criteria
- Shell sessions operate within explicit workspace scope and cwd rules.
- Reads, writes, and command-visible paths cannot silently escape the declared workspace.

**Resolution:** ## Resolution

Implemented workspace boundary enforcement across two new modules under `src/workspace/`:

### Files added

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `boundary.rs` | `WorkspaceBoundary` — declared root + `contains()`, `normalize()`, `validate_cwd()`, `validate()`; `WorkspaceError` (RootNotFound, PathEscapesBoundary, Io) |
| `cwd.rs` | `WorkspaceCwd` — workspace-scoped CWD tracker; `chdir()` enforces boundary before changing directory; `display_path()` shows `~/`-relative format |

### Integration

- `ShellRuntime` now owns a `WorkspaceBoundary` initialized from `workspace_root`
- `dispatch_cd()` validates the target path against `workspace.contains()` BEFORE calling `env.chdir()` — silently escaping the workspace is prevented
- Non-existent paths bypass the boundary check (canonicalize would fail) and fall through to the normal `DirNotFound` error
- `workspace_root()` accessor surfaces the workspace root in session state

### Test changes

- Updated `cd_absolute_path_changes_cwd` — previously cd'd to parent of temp_dir (now outside workspace); updated to cd to workspace root itself
- Added `cd_outside_workspace_is_denied`, `cd_outside_workspace_writes_error_to_transcript`, `workspace_root_accessible_via_runtime`

### Verification

- `cargo clippy --all-targets --no-deps -- -D warnings`: 0 errors, 0 warnings
- `cargo test`: 126 passed (22 new: 12 boundary + 7 cwd + 3 runtime), 0 failed

Exit criteria

## [UX-01] Build the shell cockpit UI around the embedded terminal session
**Status:** DONE
**Files:** `docs/architecture.md`, `src/ui/layout/mod.rs`, `src/ui/layout/chrome.rs`, `src/ui/layout/footer.rs`, `src/ui/layout/atoms.rs`, `src/ui/layout/panels/mod.rs`, `src/ui/layout/panels/workspace.rs`, `src/ui/layout/panels/activity.rs`, `src/ui/layout/panels/terminal.rs`, `src/ui/layout/panels/terminal_model.rs`, `src/session/events.rs`, `src/session/engine.rs`, `src/shell/runtime.rs`, `src/shell/runtime_builtins.rs`, `src/commands/registry.rs`, `src/commands/registry_data.rs`, `src/policy/engine.rs`, `src/policy/engine_tests.rs`
**Labels:** v0-1, ui, workstation, shell-cockpit, legibility
**Recorded:** 2026-04-10T22:28:39.4277954Z
**Closed:** 2026-04-11T07:40:37.912427458Z
**Depends on:** [APP-01], [SHEL-01], [SES-01]

Goal
- Build the shell cockpit UI around the embedded terminal session.

Why
- ROY should feel like a control cockpit around a controlled shell, not just a transcript window.

Scope
- Render a shell-centered center pane plus visible workspace context, session metadata, live activity, artifacts, and approval surfaces.
- Show the terminal as one product surface while keeping the machine state legible around it.
- Make the host identity obvious in layout and copy.

Exit criteria
- A user can follow an embedded-agent shell session while also seeing ROY's surrounding control surfaces.
- The UI makes clear that the terminal is part of the product, not the whole product.

**Resolution:** What changed:
- Rebuilt the cockpit around a live session signal instead of placeholder UI. The header, workspace panel, activity feed, artifact strip, diagnostics pane, and terminal pane now all read live runtime/session state.
- Wired shell input submission through the Dioxus terminal pane into `ShellRuntime::dispatch()`, drained stdout/stderr back into the transcript, and recorded typed session events for command input, command output, denials, cwd changes, and session exit.
- Split oversized UI/runtime/policy/registry files into sidecars (`terminal.rs`, `terminal_model.rs`, `runtime_builtins.rs`, `engine_tests.rs`, `registry_data.rs`) so the tree passes neti locality/size checks cleanly.

Why:
- UX-01 requires ROY to read as a shell cockpit instead of a transcript-only window. Making the surrounding machine state visible is the product boundary: the shell stays central, but workspace scope, session state, policy posture, and trace surfaces remain legible.
- Recording typed session events now gives the cockpit a durable activity model that matches the architecture and supports downstream issues like artifacts, diagnostics, and embedded-agent hosting.

Verification:
- `cargo test` -> PASS
- `cargo clippy --all-targets --no-deps -- -D warnings` -> PASS
- `neti check` -> PASS; locality clean, scan clean

Handoff:
- This clears the cockpit baseline for `AGEN-01`, `AGEN-02`, `AGEN-03`, `ART-01`, `DIAG-01`, and `UX-02` by giving those issues a live shell-centered UI surface and typed session activity to extend.

- Make denials, approvals, and compatibility traps teach the ROY world instead of merely blocking it.

Why
- The shell should retrain agent behavior through clear, immediate feedback rather than opaque failure.

Scope
- Render blocked-command attempts, approval pauses, and compatibility-trap results with explicit reasons and suggested alternatives.
- Make policy outcomes visible both in the shell flow and in the surrounding UI.
- Ensure the UI language reinforces that ROY is a controlled shell with preferred paths.

Exit criteria
- A blocked or gated action teaches what exists in ROY instead of feeling like random breakage.
- Approval state can pause and resume useful work without losing shell-session clarity.

- Store artifact references in the session ledger and render dedicated viewers in the UI.
- Let the shell flow stay readable while significant outputs are promoted into inspectable objects.

Exit criteria
- Important outputs from shell-hosted sessions can be inspected as typed artifacts instead of transcript sludge.
- The UI can directly open at least diff, validation, and denied-command-trace artifacts.

- Add shell-native help and discoverability builtins for the ROY command world.

- Since ROY denies old habits, it must make the new world easy to learn from inside the shell itself.

Scope
- Provide obvious built-ins for listing available commands, describing ROY-native capabilities, understanding workspace scope, and learning replacements for denied habits.
- Ensure denial responses can point toward these help surfaces directly.
- Keep discoverability reachable to terminal-native agents in a natural way.

Exit criteria
- An embedded agent can discover what commands and capabilities exist without falling back to blind probing.
- Denied habits have an obvious in-shell path toward the intended alternatives.

## [V-01] Ship a first convincing ROY vertical slice for v0.1
**Status:** BACKLOG
**Files:** `docs/concept.md`, `docs/architecture.md`, `src/main.rs`, `src/shell/mod.rs`, `src/agents/claude_code.rs`, `src/policy/engine.rs`, `src/ui/layout.rs`

Goal
- Ship a first convincing ROY vertical slice for v0.1 as a controlled shell host.

Why
- v0.1 should prove the thesis end to end: a real terminal-native agent can inhabit ROY's shell, hit ROY's borders, discover ROY-native commands, and still complete a simple task.

Scope
- Wire the shell host, command world, policy enforcement, workspace boundaries, embedded-agent supervision, diagnostics, artifacts, storage, and cockpit UI into one coherent slice.
- Use Claude Code as the first real embedded terminal agent for the proof.
- Tighten copy and interaction polish until the product clearly feels like a valid shell whose universe is under ROY's control.

Exit criteria
- A fresh user can watch Claude Code run inside ROY, encounter informative ROY denials and help surfaces, and complete a simple repo task.
- The demo clearly shows that ROY is hosting the agent and shaping the world rather than proxying a model API.

**Resolution:**

Scope
- Implement typed capability contracts and the first command families for workspace inspection, file mutation, validation, and other high-value host actions.
- Return structured outputs internally even when rendered as readable terminal text.
- Keep the first command set intentionally small but strong enough to support a real simple task.

Exit criteria
- ROY-native commands can inspect, mutate, and validate work without delegating to a generic shell substrate.
- Command execution results are structured enough for policy, artifacts, and UI surfaces.

Goal
- Add a developer diagnostics pane for shell traces, embedded-agent IO boundaries, and policy decisions.

Why
- Building a controlled shell host without deep introspection would be miserable. We need to see exactly how the embedded agent experiences the world.

Scope
- Expose raw command resolution, argument payloads, policy outcomes, blocked-command attempts, embedded-agent IO boundaries, and capability execution traces in a hidden or developer-only pane.
- Make it easy to correlate shell-visible behavior with internal host events.
- Keep the diagnostics surface clearly outside the embedded agent's world.

Exit criteria
- A developer can inspect a shell session end to end, including how a command was resolved and why a policy decision happened.
- The diagnostics pane materially reduces guesswork while building the host.

**Resolution:**

**Labels:** v0-1, foundation, shell-host, terminal, compatibility
**Recorded:** 2026-04-10T22:47:10.8541379Z
**Closed:** 2026-04-11T06:50:04.255274614Z
**Depends on:** [APP-01]

Goal
- Implement the compatibility shell host runtime that gives terminal-native agents a valid world to inhabit.

Why
- The shell host is now the center of the product. If ROY does not own the terminal environment, it is only wrapping a shell instead of replacing one.

Scope
- Provide terminal-facing runtime behavior for stdin/stdout, current working directory semantics, environment variables, exit statuses, command lookup, and supervised execution flow.
- Shape PATH and command availability through ROY rather than blind delegation to the operating system.
- Return fast, deterministic failures for unsupported surfaces so the environment teaches itself clearly.

Exit criteria
- ROY can host an interactive shell session with consistent IO, exit statuses, and command lookup.
- The shell runtime is valid enough for an installed terminal-native agent to begin interacting with it.

**Resolution:** ## Resolution

Implemented the ROY shell host runtime across six new modules under `src/shell/`:

### Modules added

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `env.rs` | `ShellEnv` — controlled PATH/CWD/var store; excludes all OS PATH entries |
| `io.rs` | `ShellIo` trait + `BufferedIo` — session transcript accumulator |
| `result.rs` | `DispatchResult` — typed outcome enum for every command dispatch |
| `traps.rs` | `COMPAT_TRAPS` — 25-entry static table of denied shell fallback commands |
| `runtime.rs` | `ShellRuntime` — dispatch engine: built-ins → compat traps → NotFound |
| `runtime_tests_builtins.rs` | 13 sidecar tests for pwd/cd/env/exit/help/drain |
| `runtime_tests_policy.rs` | 11 sidecar tests for traps, NotFound, prompt indicators |

### Key design decisions

- **Controlled PATH**: `ShellEnv::roy_path()` returns `/usr/local/lib/roy/bin` only — no `/usr/bin`, `/bin`, etc. This is the primary mechanism preventing shell-shaped fallback.
- **Sidecar test files**: `runtime.rs` approached the 2000-token advisory limit, so tests were split into two `#[path = "..."]` sidecar modules.
- **`#![allow(dead_code)]`**: Shell API is foundational infrastructure not yet fully wired into `main()`. Suppressions are scoped per-file and documented with pending issue references (SHEL-02, TOOL-01).
- **`is_none_or` for env filter**: Replaced `filter.map_or(true, ...)` with `filter.is_none_or(...)` per clippy suggestion.

### Verification

- `cargo clippy --all-targets --no-deps -- -D warnings`: 0 errors, 0 warnings
- `cargo test`: 38 passed, 0 failed

## [AGEN-01] Define the embedded-agent adapter and supervised process contract
**Status:** BACKLOG
**Files:** `docs/architecture.md`, `src/agents/mod.rs`, `src/agents/adapter.rs`, `src/agents/session.rs`, `src/shell/mod.rs`
**Labels:** v0-1, foundation, agents, embedding, process-supervision
**Recorded:** 2026-04-10T22:47:10.9657078Z
**Depends on:** [SHEL-01], [SES-01], [POL-01]

Goal
- Define the embedded-agent adapter and supervised process contract for terminal-native agents.

Why
- ROY's primary integration target is now installed agent products such as Claude Code and Codex, not model APIs.

Scope
- Define how ROY installs, launches, supervises, and records embedded terminal-agent sessions.
- Handle stdin/stdout attachment, session ownership, authentication handoff hooks, version metadata, and lifecycle supervision.
- Treat embedded agents as known tenants of the shell host rather than arbitrary child binaries.

Exit criteria
- ROY can launch and supervise an embedded agent through a stable host contract.
- Agent IO, lifecycle events, and host observations can be attached to durable session traces.

**Resolution:**
